pages.extract: Dark matter is a hypothetical form of matter thought to account for approximately 85% of the matter in the universe.
The existence of dark matter is inferred from gravitational effects on visible matter and the structure of the cosmos.
Dark matter has not been directly observed, but its presence is suggested through gravitational effects on galaxies and galaxy clusters.
The term 'dark matter' refers to matter that does not interact with electromagnetic radiation, such as light.
Scientists believe dark matter forms a network of cosmic filaments throughout the universe, shaping the distribution of galaxies.
The search for dark matter particles involves sophisticated detectors deep underground to shield from cosmic rays.
Dark matter was first postulated by Swiss astronomer Fritz Zwicky in 1933 to explain the motion of galaxies in clusters.
The bullet cluster collision provides some of the strongest evidence for the existence of dark matter.
WIMPs (Weakly Interacting Massive Particles) are among the leading candidates for dark matter particles.
Understanding dark matter is crucial for explaining how galaxies form and evolve over cosmic time.
Serotonin is a neurotransmitter that plays a key role in mood regulation, happiness, and well-being.
About 90% of the body's serotonin is produced in the digestive tract, not the brain.
Low serotonin levels are associated with depression, anxiety, and sleep disorders.
Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) are common antidepressants that work by increasing serotonin levels.
Serotonin helps regulate appetite, digestion, and bone density.
Habits are formed through a loop of cue, craving, response, and reward.
It takes an average of 66 days for a new behavior to become automatic.
Small, consistent changes are more effective than dramatic transformations when building habits.
Environment design plays a crucial role in habit formation and maintenance.
Habit stacking involves linking a new habit to an existing one to increase success.
Inflammation is the body's natural response to injury or infection.
Chronic inflammation can contribute to various diseases including heart disease and cancer.
Diet, stress, and sleep quality all influence inflammation levels in the body.
Anti-inflammatory foods include leafy greens, fatty fish, and berries.
Exercise can help reduce chronic inflammation in the body.
Diaphragmatic breathing engages the body's main breathing muscle located below the lungs.
This breathing technique can help reduce stress and lower blood pressure.
Proper diaphragmatic breathing involves the belly expanding on inhale and contracting on exhale.
Regular practice of diaphragmatic breathing can improve core stability and posture.
Many people breathe incorrectly, using chest breathing instead of diaphragmatic breathing.
Photons are elementary particles that carry electromagnetic force, including light.
Photons have zero rest mass and always travel at the speed of light in a vacuum.
The energy of a photon determines its wavelength and frequency.
Photons exhibit both wave-like and particle-like properties, known as wave-particle duality.
Solar panels work by converting photons from sunlight into electrical energy.
The vagus nerve is the longest cranial nerve, extending from the brain to the abdomen.
It plays a crucial role in the parasympathetic nervous system, controlling rest and digestion.
Vagal tone can be improved through deep breathing, meditation, and cold exposure.
The vagus nerve influences heart rate, digestion, immune response, and mood.
Vagus nerve stimulation is used as a treatment for epilepsy and depression.
Gluten is a protein found in wheat, barley, rye, and some other grains.
Celiac disease is an autoimmune disorder triggered by gluten consumption.
Non-celiac gluten sensitivity can cause similar symptoms to celiac disease.
Gluten gives bread its elastic texture and helps it rise during baking.
Many ancient grains like quinoa and amaranth are naturally gluten-free.
Black holes are regions of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing can escape, not even light.
The boundary of a black hole is called the event horizon.
Supermassive black holes exist at the center of most galaxies, including our Milky Way.
Black holes form when massive stars die and collapse under their own gravity.
Time appears to slow down near a black hole's event horizon due to extreme gravitational effects.
